content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Demographically-driven passive contraction (status-quo baseline)'. Delaying academic selection beyond age 12 in Hungary and extending common schooling to age 16, bundled with university-based teacher-education reform and protected against school-choice-driven substitution of formal tracking with within-school segregation, would be expected to reduce achievement gaps and raise intergenerational mobility, but only if the full policy package is implemented coherently.
opportunities: Provides a clean counterfactual baseline against which the incremental effects of S1-S4 can be measured, isolating policy-driven change from demographically-driven change.

Reduces the absolute number of selective-track places over time simply through cohort shrinkage, without requiring any legislative or administrative action, avoiding all the implementation and reversal risks documented for S1-S4.
strengths: Finland's 1972–1977 comprehensive school reform delayed tracking from age 11 to age 16, eliminating early selection into vocational and general tracks.
Delaying tracking to age 16 reduced intergenerational income elasticity by 23%, from 0.30 to 0.23, indicating substantial gains in social mobility.
threats: Politically the path of least resistance, since it requires no legislative action and therefore faces no direct Fidesz culture-war framing risk, but this apparent political safety is itself a risk: it allows policymakers to avoid the equity problem indefinitely while claiming demographic forces are 'naturally' solving selectivity, when the evidence shows government is simultaneously using score thresholds (S2) as an active supply lever layered atop passive contraction.

Absence of active intervention risks EU-level criticism given the European Commission's explicit 2025 finding that Hungary's system amplifies social and economic inequalities, potentially triggering funds-conditionality pressure regardless of demographic trends.
weaknesses: The causal contribution of tracking-age delay alone versus the full Finnish policy bundle (welfare expansion, teacher-education upgrade, curricular change) remains uncertain; isolating the tracking effect requires counterfactual scenarios not available from Finnish data.
